retrieved_docs.title: Biliary Obstruction -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Normal urine contains no bilirubin; however, in patients with obstructive jaundice, conjugated bilirubin is excreted, giving it a dark color. Urinary bilirubin is detectable at a lower level than that needed to cause clinical jaundice.

retrieved_docs.title: Biliary Obstruction -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Normal total serum bilirubin values are 0.2 to 1.2 mg/dL. Clinically, jaundice is evident at 3 mg/dl.

retrieved_docs.title: Biliary Obstruction -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The inability of bilirubin to reach the intestinal tract gives a pale color to stools.

retrieved_docs.title: Biliary Stenting -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Pancreatic head cancer and cholangiocarcinoma can lead to obstructive jaundice. While benign diseases may also cause obstructive jaundice, they can still have deleterious effects if not treated promptly. Obstructive jaundice can result in hepatocellular dysfunction, biliary cirrhosis, and an increased risk of cholangitis.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Biliary -- Pathophysiology -- Biliary Tract Disorders
retrieved_docs.content: Biliary atresia [34] Progressive fibrosing obstructive cholangiopathy of the intrahepatic and extrahepatic biliary system, leading to obstruction of bile flow and neonatal jaundice.

retrieved_docs.title: Biliary Obstruction -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Jaundice, a physical exam finding of yellowish discoloration of the skin, conjunctiva, or mucous membranes, is a consequence of obstruction leading to bile stasis and buildup of conjugated bilirubin in the blood.

retrieved_docs.title: Postcholecystectomy Syndrome -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The pathophysiology is related to alterations in bile flow suggested by the removal of the reservoir function of the gallbladder altering bile flow. Bile is thought to be the main trigger in patients with mild gastroduodenal symptoms or diarrhea. While our current knowledge about the pathophysiology of post-cholecystectomy syndrome has improved since the introduction of endoscopic retrograde cholangiopancreatography (ERCP) and endoscopic sphincter of Oddi manometry (ESOM) in diagnostic circumstances, the pathophysiology is not yet fully understood. Post-cholecystectomy syndrome is thought to be caused by organic or functional disease of the gastrointestinal tract and can be further classified into patients with symptoms of non-biliary and biliary origin for systematic evaluation.

retrieved_docs.content: Prognosis of jaundice depends on the etiology.

retrieved_docs.title: Bilirubinuria -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: It is also helpful to categorize the differential as hepatocellular injury versus cholestatic causes. Weight loss and constitutional symptoms are consistent with obstructive malignancy, or a history of immune deficiency, which can cause biliary obstruction from opportunistic infections. Patients with biliary obstruction might also complain of dark brown urine, light-colored stool, or pruritis. [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Bile -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The biliary system pathology includes autoimmune diseases, obstructive cholestasis, non-obstructive cholestasis, infections, and malignancy. The autoimmune diseases are primary biliary cholangitis (PBC) and primary sclerosing cholangitis. There is autoimmune-mediated progressive destruction of intrahepatic small-medium-sized bile ducts in primary biliary cholangitis, resulting in impaired bile secretion and intrahepatic cholestasis. [10] There is intrahepatic and extrahepatic destruction of bile ducts in primary sclerosing cholangitis. PSC is strongly associated with ulcerative colitis. The autoimmune basis for both conditions (PBC and PSC) is present in the histologic findings and specific antibodies in patients with these 2 conditions. Moreover, both conditions are often associated with other autoimmune diseases, further supporting the autoimmune basis.

retrieved_docs.content: Occult blood to rule out GI malignancy Radiological and Endoscopic tests - Non-invasive and invasive radiologic tests can help diagnose the underlying etiology of biliary obstruction. [15] [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Biliary Obstruction -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Biliary obstruction refers to the impairment of bile flow from the liver to the small intestine due to blockage of the biliary duct system. Bile obstruction affects a large portion of the population worldwide, with significant morbidity and mortality. The most common etiology of biliary obstruction is gallstones causing common bile duct obstruction, which manifests as pain, nausea/vomiting, and jaundice. This activity describes the etiology, pathophysiology, evaluation, and management of biliary obstruction and highlights the interprofessional team's role in caring for affected patients.

retrieved_docs.content: The differential diagnosis for biliary obstruction is broad, ranging from benign to malignant diseases, as mentioned below.

retrieved_docs.title: Bouveret Syndrome (Bilioduodenal Fistula) -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Unfortunately, laboratory studies are typically non-specific. Labs may show jaundice and hepatic enzyme alterations, but this is only seen in about one-third of the patients with Bouveret syndrome. Leucocytosis, electrolyte abnormalities, acid-base alterations, and renal failure may also be present, but the grade depends on the comorbidity, the intensity of the inflammatory response, and the compensatory mechanisms of the individual. As far as imaging is concerned, the constellation of pneumobilia, bowel obstruction, and an aberrant gallstone referred to as the Rigler triad is highly suggestive of Bouveret syndrome but is only found in 40% to 50% of cases (see Image. Bouveret Syndrome With Rigler Triad). Ultrasound may be useful, showing possible cholecystitis, dilated stomach, pneumobilia, and ectopic location of gallstone, yet bowel gas makes it suboptimal. If the gallbladder is contracted, it may be difficult to detect the exact location of the stone (orthotopic or ectopic) using ultrasound. [19] [20] [21]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 4 -- Community 2812
retrieved_docs.content: The term “Allowed qualifier” is recognized as an etiological descriptor for both ureteral obstruction (انسداد الحالب) and extrahepatic biliary obstruction (انسداد صفراوي خارج الكبد), indicating that this qualifier is considered a permissible cause or contributing factor for these obstructive conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 5 -- Community 2957
retrieved_docs.content: Allowed qualifier is identified as an etiological descriptor for several medical conditions. It is linked to the etiology of ولادة قبل الأوان (premature birth), قَرْحَة مَعِدِيّة (gastric ulcer), انسداد صفراوي خارج الكبد (extrahepatic biliary obstruction), and الالتصاق (adhesion). Recognizing these allowed qualifiers helps clinicians accurately attribute causes to these diverse conditions, facilitating clearer documentation and more precise patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 1724
retrieved_docs.content: The medical concept “Allowed qualifier” is recognized as a possible complication in several distinct conditions. It may develop as a complication of ureteral obstruction (انسداد الحالب), of acute cholestasis of the liver (ضُمورُ الكَبِد الأصفر الحادّ), and of a smooth muscle tumor (ورم عضلي أملس). Recognizing “Allowed qualifier” as a complication across these diverse pathologies can aid clinicians in monitoring for its occurrence and implementing appropriate management strategies.

The SIGN SYMPTOM COMPLEX is clinically associated with chest radiography (الأشعة السينية على الصَدْر), emphasizing the role of imaging in the evaluation of these interconnected vascular, hepatic, gastrointestinal and neurological disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 4 -- Community 2892
retrieved_docs.content: The allowed qualifier is recognized as an appropriate therapeutic option for managing diabetes during pregnancy, acute cholestasis of the liver, and fetal distress syndrome, indicating its relevance across these distinct obstetric and hepatic conditions and its role in guiding patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 4 -- Community 2805
retrieved_docs.content: Allowed qualifier is recognized as an appropriate descriptor for both the epidemiology and the etiology of a range of conditions, including ولادة قبل الأوان (preterm birth), تمزق (rupture), قَرْحَة مَعِدِيّة (gastric ulcer), اكتئاب (depression), انسداد صفراوي خارج الكبد (extra‑hepatic biliary obstruction), ضُمورُ الكَبِد الأصفر الحادّ (acute cholestasis of the liver), عدوى بفيروس معوي (enteric viral infection) and الالتصاق (adhesion). By being applicable to the study of how these disorders occur in populations (epidemiology) and the underlying causes (etiology), the allowed qualifier facilitates consistent classification and analysis across diverse medical topics.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2861
retrieved_docs.content: The designation “Allowed qualifier” is applicable as a qualifier for both enzymology and etiology across a spectrum of disorders, including gastric ulcer (قَرْحَة مَعِدِيّة), iron‑deficiency anemia (فقر الدم الناتج عن نقص الحديد), ureteral obstruction (انسداد الحالب), acute cholestasis of the liver (ضُمورُ الكَبِد الأصفر الحادّ), smooth muscle tumor (ورم عضلي أملس), and other disorders of the stomach and duodenum. Using this allowed qualifier enables precise specification of the enzymatic mechanisms and underlying causes for each condition, thereby enhancing clarity in clinical documentation, research analysis, and patient‑care planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 1881
retrieved_docs.content: انسداد (obstruction) is linked to several related medical entities. It is associated with خانُوق, رُّكودِ صفْراوِيّ, and عِلَّوس, indicating that obstruction commonly co‑occurs with or contributes to these conditions. In addition, انسداد has a subordinate condition termed انفتال, suggesting a hierarchical relationship in which انفتال represents a specific manifestation or complication of obstruction. Furthermore, انسداد is directly related to انسداد المثانة (bladder obstruction), highlighting that bladder obstruction is a particular form of the broader obstruction concept. Understanding these interconnections helps clinicians recognize the spectrum of disorders associated with obstruction and guides comprehensive assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 2324
retrieved_docs.content: Bacterial sepsis of the newborn, unspecified, is classified as a neonatal condition. The qualifier “Neonatal (qualifier value)” is used to denote a range of disorders that occur in the newborn period, including several distinct types of neonatal jaundice: neonatal jaundice due to delayed conjugation from delayed development of the conjugating system, نَزْف المسالك التنفسية الوَليدِيّ (neonatal respiratory distress), يرقان حليب الثدي (breast‑milk jaundice), يرقان نووي ناجم عن التمنيع الإسوي لدى الأجنة أو المواليد (jaundice resulting from immature hepatic conjugation in fetuses or newborns), and يرقان وليدي مرتبط بالولادة قبل الأوان (prematurity‑associated jaundice). These interrelated neonatal entities share the common context of early‑life physiology and pathology, underscoring the importance of recognizing their overlapping presentations for accurate diagnosis and appropriate management in newborn care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 198
retrieved_docs.content: outcomes, fetal growth disturbances, a spectrum of neonatal infections, genetic syndromes, bleeding events, various forms of jaundice, and traumatic injuries, providing a comprehensive framework for perinatal assessment, diagnosis, and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 4 -- Community 2789
retrieved_docs.content: The term “Allowed qualifier” is applicable as a descriptive qualifier when discussing both the epidemiology and the etiology of a range of conditions, including ولادة قبل الأوان (premature birth), قَرْحَة مَعِدِيّة (gastric ulcer), حَصاة (stone), انسداد صفراوي خارج الكبد (extra‑hepatic biliary obstruction), other disorders of stomach and duodenum, and تهديد بالإجهاض (threatened abortion). In each of these disorders, the “Allowed qualifier” may be used to qualify statements about how frequently the condition occurs in populations (epidemiology) and the underlying causes or contributing factors (etiology). This consistent use across diverse gastrointestinal, obstetric, and neonatal conditions underscores the flexibility of the qualifier in clinical documentation and research reporting.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 2268
retrieved_docs.content: The neonatal qualifier value encompasses several newborn conditions. It includes umbilical bleeding after birth, which can occur when a loose umbilical cord clamp leads to bleeding. It also occurs in various forms of neonatal jaundice, such as breast‑milk jaundice, physiologic jaundice that results from immature liver function in fetuses or newborns, and jaundice that is linked to premature delivery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 2 -- Community 1163
retrieved_docs.content: The qualifier “Neonatal (qualifier value)” designates conditions that manifest during the newborn period. It is applied to the occurrence of نزف سري بعد الولادة (umbilical bleeding after birth), نَزْف المسالك التنفسية الوَليدِيّ (neonatal respiratory tract bleeding), يرقان حليب الثدي (breast‑milk jaundice), يرقان نووي (physiologic neonatal jaundice), and يرقان نووي ناجم عن التمنيع الإسوي لدى الأجنة أو المواليد (physiologic jaundice resulting from immature hepatic conjugation in fetuses or newborns). Recognizing these entities as neonatal helps clinicians focus assessment and management on disorders specific to the early post‑natal timeframe.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2341
retrieved_docs.content: Failure (biologic function) is associated with الفَشَلٌ الكَبِدِيُّ الكُلْوِيّ, indicating that a general loss of biological function can be linked to hepatorenal failure—a severe condition in which both liver and kidney functions are compromised. Recognizing this association is important for clinicians, as it highlights the need to assess and manage systemic organ failure comprehensively in affected patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 869
retrieved_docs.content: Neonatal (qualifier value) serves as a broad category that includes several distinct neonatal conditions. Within this category, neonatal jaundice due to delayed conjugation is recognized as a specific form of jaundice that manifests in the newborn period. Additional conditions that occur in the neonatal timeframe include نَزْف المسالك التنفسية الوَليدِيّ (neonatal respiratory tract hemorrhage), يرقان حليب الثدي (breast‑milk jaundice), يرقان نووي (physiologic or nuclear jaundice), and يرقان وليدي مرتبط بالولادة قبل الأوان (prematurity‑related neonatal jaundice). Together, these entities illustrate the variety of etiologies—ranging from delayed bilirubin conjugation and breast‑milk factors to prematurity and respiratory bleeding—that can affect newborns, underscoring the importance of recognizing each specific presentation for accurate diagnosis and appropriate neonatal care.
